## [00:00 - 03:03]

Alright guys, let's have a look at the four pillar creative framework, so 4p creative framework. This is basically, especially when you're doing a rapid fire test, you're going to have to roll out some ad creative in each of those ad sets, right? And ideally, all of those ad sets will have the same ad creative if you're testing out the audience to reduce any variables in that test. But how do you know which kind of creative to roll out or what to test out? So this is the framework that we use inside of the agency, and essentially what it is, is we try to knock out all four of these within the test. So we're going to try to have single image. So these are different ad formats. So basically the 4p framework is about attacking different ad formats, and then we're also going to look at some different themes behind those as well. So we will always roll out a single image in one of those. A carousel ad, in fact, a lot of the times we use the dynamic carousels now, which is almost like DPA style. This one requires a product catalog, so just be aware of that. One ad format, because this can work really, really well top of funnel for e-commerce. Again, you're going to need a product catalog for that one. And then video, because obviously all four of these can do really, really well. So what we're trying to figure out is which audience is going to perform the best. However, we want to make sure that our creative is going to be pretty good, that something in there is going to appeal to the audience or trigger the audience really well. So some of the best practices before I take you guys some of the themes that you can roll out in terms of the creative, I would always include a link to the product page or to the site in the copy. And I would start telling your client that everything they post to their page should also include a link in there so that you can potentially test their posts out in the actual funnel as well. I would mention hook in the headline. So if you do have a hook like free shipping or it's a sale or 50% off, I would mention that in the headline. Emojis do help as well. They do get you a higher click-through rate, so just be aware of that. And you do want to have some social proof. That's important for e-commerce. People want what other people want or have, right? And so when we're looking at social proof, normally what you're going to see is ads that are proofed by about a thousand to 40,000 tend to get the best results. And that's purely because of that peer pressure, the social proof assigned to that. So some of the most common creative approaches for e-commerce specifically that works really

## [03:03 - 06:08]

well. So unboxing videos top of funnel. I couldn't actually find an ad shot for that, so that's not really an unboxing video, that one. An unboxing video is usually something that's super native. It's basically a video of someone opening up the product as it arrives in their place. And that's to show people the quality, the packaging, et cetera, that it is a legitimate thing and that it's high quality. So those work really, really well. Influencers and user-generated content can also do really well. So if your client did do some stuff with some influencers or they have an Instagram and a lot of people are tagging them in, pull some of those creatives in, just don't forget to mention the people that created that content. Product shots, this is the most common one. So this could be, again, a single image, it could be a video, but essentially the main focus is the actual product. And then the lifestyle approach, where it's not so much the product shot, it's more the lifestyle around it. So for example, if you were selling some sneakers, it wouldn't be a shot focused on the sneakers. It would be maybe a woman skateboarding wearing the sneakers. So it's aspirational and it tries to position people into the branding, the image that they assign to themselves.
